saida_bruta:
“Your face looks familiar,” he said politely. “Weren’t you with the First Division in the war?”

“Why yes. I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was in the Sixteenth until June 1918. I knew I’d seen you somewhere before.”

We spoke briefly of a few damp, gray villages in France. Clearly he lived nearby, for he mentioned he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to test it in the morning.

“Want to join me, old sport? Just along the shore by the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Whenever suits you best.”

I nearly asked his name when Jordan turned and smiled.

“Having a good time now?” she asked.

“Much better.” I faced my new acquaintance again. “This party feels strange to me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I gestured toward the unseen hedge in the distance, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation.”

For a moment he stared as though he did not grasp my meaning.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, I beg your pardon.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.”

saida_bruta:
“Your face looks familiar,” he remarked courteously. “Didn’t you serve with the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, indeed. I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I remained in the Sixteenth until June 1918. I was certain I’d encountered you somewhere earlier.”

We exchanged a few words about several damp, gray villages in France. Evidently he lived close by, since he noted he had recently purchased a hydroplane and intended to try it out the next morning.

“Care to come along, old sport? Simply up the shore by the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Any hour that suits you.”

I almost inquired his name just as Jordan turned and smiled.

“Enjoying yourself more now?” she inquired.

“Far better.” I turned back to my new companion. “This gathering still seems odd to me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I pointed toward the distant, unseen hedge—“and this Gatsby fellow dispatched his chauffeur with an invitation.”

For an instant he gazed at me as if the words made no sense.

“I’m Gatsby,” he announced abruptly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I assumed you realized, old sport. I fear I’m rather a poor host.”

“Your face looks familiar,” he remarked courteously. “Did you serve with the First Division in the war?”

“Yes, I did. I was assigned to the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I stayed with the Sixteenth until June 1918. I was sure we had encountered each other before.”

We spoke briefly of assorted damp, gray villages in France. He clearly resided nearby, mentioning he had lately purchased a hydroplane and planned to test it the following morning.

“Would you like to join me, old sport? Just along the shore by the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Whenever is convenient for you.”

I was on the verge of inquiring his name when Jordan turned and smiled.

“Enjoying yourself more now?” she asked.

“Much better.” I turned back to my new acquaintance. “This party still seems odd to me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I pointed to the distant, invisible hedge—“and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation.”

For an instant he regarded me as if the words were incomprehensible.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, do forgive me.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.

“Much better.” I turned again to my new acquaintance. “This party still strikes me as strange. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I pointed toward the distant, unseen hedge—“and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation.”

For a moment he looked at me as though the words made no sense.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.”
saida_filtrada:
Your face looks familiar,” he observed politely. “Did you serve with the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I did. I was assigned to the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I remained with the Sixteenth until June 1918. I felt certain we had met before.”

We talked briefly of various damp, gray villages in France. He evidently lived nearby, noting he had recently bought a hydroplane and meant to try it out the next morning.

“Would you care to join me, old sport? Just along the shore by the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Any time that suits you.”

I was about to ask his name when Jordan turned and smiled.

“Enjoying yourself more now?” she inquired.

“Much better.” I turned again to my new acquaintance. “This party still strikes me as strange. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I pointed toward the distant, unseen hedge—“and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation.”

For a moment he looked at me as though the words made no sense.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I cried. “Oh, please forgive me.”

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not a very good host.
